source.title: Tank Explosions at Midland Resource Recovery

event.costs: $19,013 OSHA penalties to MRR; $12,675 OSHA penalty to SPSI
actions_taken: CSB deployed investigative team on May 28, 2017
MRR hired SPSI to conduct investigation and mitigation work after May 24 explosion
SPSI developed a protocol to drain remaining MP odorizers while avoiding hydrogen generation conditions
MRR hired SRS to vent and drain remaining 11 MP odorizers after June 20 explosion
SRS subcontracted ESI to use precision explosive cutting tools to cut holes in vessels
MRR issued Public Safety Information Memo to Barbour County Sheriff's Office
MRR stopped using sodium hypochlorite and adopted a proprietary process post-incident
OSHA issued citations to MRR and SPSI following both incidents
event.summary: On May 24, 2017, an explosion at the Midland Resource Recovery (MRR) facility in Philippi, West Virginia killed two workers and severely injured a third while they were draining chemically treated Peerless MP odorizer equipment. A second explosion on June 20, 2017, fatally injured a contractor from SPSI who was attempting to drain the remaining treated odorizers. The CSB determined that the probable cause was reactive, unstable chemicals formed when uncharacterized vessel contents reacted with sodium hypochlorite used to remove mercaptan odor from decommissioned natural gas odorizer equipment. MRR lacked a formal hazard identification process, process safety management system, overpressure protection on the vessels, written procedures for MP odorizer treatment, and had not tested vessel contents prior to chemical treatment.
recommendations: OSHA recommendation 2001-01-H-R1: Amend PSM Standard (29 CFR 1910.119) to achieve more comprehensive control of reactive hazards, broaden application to cover reactive hazards from process-specific conditions and combinations of chemicals, require multiple sources of information in process safety information compilation, and augment PHA element to explicitly require evaluation of reactive hazards.

EPA recommendation 2001-01-H-R3: Revise Accidental Release Prevention Requirements (40 CFR 68) to explicitly cover catastrophic reactive hazards including those from self-reactive chemicals and combinations of chemicals and process-specific conditions.

hazards.name: Reactive chemicals in sealed pressure vessels (sodium hypochlorite reacting with uncharacterized mercaptan equipment contents)
Pressurized MP odorizer vessels containing unstable chemical reaction products

threats.name: Uncharacterized vessel contents reacting with sodium hypochlorite to form explosive/unstable compounds (e.g., alkyl hypochlorites, hydrogen gas)
Absence of overpressure protection on sealed pressure vessels containing reactive liquids
Mechanical disturbance of treated MP odorizer during draining operations initiating rapid pressure rise
Prolonged contact time of sodium hypochlorite with vessel contents allowing unstable compound formation

consequences.name: Fatalities to workers (2 killed in May 24 explosion; 1 contractor killed in June 20 explosion)
Serious injury to worker (1 severely injured in May 24 explosion)
Catastrophic vessel rupture and projectile hazard
Release of mercaptan and process water causing community odor concerns and public alarm
Regulatory penalties and enforcement actions (OSHA citations)

controls.name: Chemical content testing/characterization of vessel contents prior to treatment
controls.barrier_role: Identify reactive chemical hazards before sodium hypochlorite treatment to prevent formation of explosive compounds
controls.lod_basis: First line of defense: hazard identification before initiating the chemical treatment process

supporting_text: At no point in MRR's decommissioning or chemical treatment process were the contents inside either vessel tested to determine what was initially present.
MRR technicians do not perform any testing to analyze or characterize what chemicals are present in the MP odorizer.

No compatibility studies were completed prior to treating equipment, and MRR conducted no studies to determine the exothermic rates of reaction for adding different solutions of bleach to different mercaptan odorant mixtures.

controls.name: Formal Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) / Risk Assessment for MP odorizer decommissioning
controls.barrier_role: Systematically identify and evaluate reactive chemical hazards and establish safe operating basis before commencing decommissioning operations
controls.lod_basis: First line of defense: structured hazard evaluation before process design and operation

supporting_text: No formal or documented risk assessment was conducted before MRR began its decommissioning process for odorant equipment, and no formal or documented risk assessment was done before establishing the MP odorizer decommissioning process.
No risk assessment (PHA) was conducted for the MP chemical treatment process, and no formalized risk assessment was conducted for chemically treating any vessels.

controls.name: Written operating procedures for MP odorizer chemical treatment and draining
controls.barrier_role: Provide standardized, documented steps for safely treating and draining MP odorizers to prevent deviation from safe practice
controls.lod_basis: Second line of defense: procedural control during operations

supporting_text: MRR had no formal procedure addressing how to chemically treat and drain the MP odorizers.
No written procedure was developed to detail how to chemically treat or safely decommission MP odorizers. The only procedure was for a generic storage tank.
MRR lacked controls, such as established limits for how long to keep the MP odorizers full of sodium hypochlorite to control for any unintended chemical reactions.

controls.name: Overpressure protection (pressure relief valves or rupture discs) on treated MP odorizer vessels
controls.barrier_role: Prevent catastrophic vessel rupture by relieving excess pressure generated by uncontrolled chemical reactions
controls.lod_basis: Second line of defense: engineering safeguard to limit pressure buildup

supporting_text: MRR therefore did not install pressure relief on the tanks because the company thought it was not needed.
Even though these pressure vessels were filled with reactive liquid chemicals, no overpressure protection was provided for them.
The ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code requires that all pressure vessels 'shall be provided with overpressure protection'.

MRR technicians removed these devices before transporting the equipment for chemical treatment because of concerns that the valve could be a weak point that would allow an odor event to occur should a transportation accident happen.

controls.name: Management of Change (MOC) process for deviations in chemical treatment method
controls.barrier_role: Ensure formal evaluation of safety implications when changing from standard tank treatment to MP odorizer treatment (e.g., complete filling vs. 10% fill level)
controls.lod_basis: First line of defense: organizational control to evaluate process changes before implementation

supporting_text: No formal analysis was conducted to determine how to chemically treat MP odorizers differently than single storage tanks. MP odorizers were filled completely, which was a deviation from the normal process, but no Management of Change was conducted.
Although the general tank procedure called for filling the tanks with sodium hypochlorite only up to the 10 percent level, the MP odorizers were completely filled with liquid.

controls.name: Control of sodium hypochlorite dilution and treatment duration limits
controls.barrier_role: Prevent formation of unstable reactive compounds by controlling concentration and contact time of sodium hypochlorite with vessel contents
controls.lod_basis: Second line of defense: operational parameter control during chemical treatment

supporting_text: MRR lacked controls, such as established limits for how long to keep the MP odorizers full of sodium hypochlorite to control for any unintended chemical reactions. Also, MRR did not control the sodium hypochlorite dilution.

For treating tanks, MRR normally kept the sodium hypochlorite in the tanks for only one to two days. When treating the MP odorizers, however, MRR allowed them to sit for over a month, potentially allowing unstable chemicals to form.
Workers established the dilution quantities roughly, based on estimating the volume of large, partly filled totes.

controls.name: Worker training on reactive chemical hazards of sodium hypochlorite treatment process
controls.barrier_role: Ensure workers understand reactive hazards of mixing sodium hypochlorite with mercaptans, methanol, diesel, or kerosene to prevent inadvertent creation of explosive conditions
controls.lod_basis: Second line of defense: human barrier through knowledge and awareness

supporting_text: Other MRR employees were either unaware of the potential for methanol, diesel, or kerosene to be present in the odorization equipment or were unaware that these chemicals could form explosive compounds when mixed with sodium hypochlorite.
MRR's vice president of operations said, 'I'm not aware of any' [concerns about methanol, diesel, or kerosene in the odorization equipment].
Integration of reactive hazard information into process safety information, operating procedures, training, and communication practices is vital to handling reactive chemicals safely.

controls.name: Physical barriers / exclusion zones during draining operations
controls.barrier_role: Protect workers from projectile hazards in the event of vessel rupture during draining operations
controls.lod_basis: Recovery/mitigation: physical protection to reduce consequence severity if explosion occurs

supporting_text: During the nearly five minutes from when SPSI started their activities to drain the process water from the MP odorizer up to the time of the explosion, only one of the four SPSI employees consistently remained behind the concrete barrier wall.
MRR and SPSI needed to provide more barriers and strengthen their work plan for these concrete barriers to properly serve as a worker protection safeguard.

Had MRR and SPSI established a more robust barrier system, such barriers may have helped to protect the SPSI field supervisor from being struck and fatally injured by the projectile when the MP chamber exploded.

The protocol does not mention the concrete barriers, establish any type of exclusion zone near the MP odorizer, or suggest that SPSI workers would use the concrete barriers as a safeguard while performing the work.

controls.name: Pressure monitoring during draining operations with defined shutdown criteria
controls.barrier_role: Detect abnormal pressure buildup and halt draining operations before vessel rupture
controls.lod_basis: Second line of defense: detection and response to abnormal conditions during draining

supporting_text: SPSI had installed an additional (new) valve and pressure gauge into the piping coming off the middle chamber. SPSI's procedure called for halting the operation if the pressure gauge showed more than 30 psig. Because the pressure gauge showed no pressure, the operation continued.
One SPSI witness explained, '[I] saw the line kick a little bit, which was sort of a surprise…because the gauge had said zero [pressure].'
The pressure gauge indicated zero pressure in the MP odorizer.

controls.name: Explosive venting / remote vessel breach method for draining reactive vessels
controls.barrier_role: Drain vessels remotely using explosive charges to eliminate need for personnel proximity to hazardous vessels during draining
controls.lod_basis: Recovery: engineering method to safely remediate remaining hazardous vessels after incidents

supporting_text: The use of explosive venting of the vessels at MRR provides the safest means to reduce the risk of another incident. Both incidents have resulted after manual manipulation, of some kind, of valves or fittings on the vessels.

ESI prepared an Explosive Blast Plan for MRR that explained how it would conduct this work. ESI's plan was to use proprietary precision explosive cutting tools to cut two-inch holes in each vessel to allow SRS to conduct a final remediation procedure at the site.

SPSI had initially planned to use linear shaped charge explosives to cut a hole in the vessels, allowing the process water to empty directly to the ground, avoiding the need for people to be near the MP odorizers during the draining process.

controls.name: Verification of vessel liquid content before draining (sight glass inspection)
controls.barrier_role: Confirm presence or absence of liquid in vessel chambers before initiating draining to anticipate pressure conditions
controls.lod_basis: Second line of defense: pre-task check to verify vessel state before draining

supporting_text: SPSI first tried to drain an MP odorizer that SPSI personnel believed, based on not seeing liquid through the sight glass, did not have any liquid in its second chamber.
SPSI, however, had not expected liquid to be in the middle chamber because when looking through the sight glass during their planning activities the workers did not seen any liquid.

The contractor could not see any liquid under the aluminum tray when looking into the vessel through the sight glass, but the tray may have been blocking the view of a small amount of liquid inside the second chamber.

controls.name: Process Safety Management (PSM) system covering reactive chemical hazards
controls.barrier_role: Provide overarching management framework to identify, evaluate, and control reactive chemical hazards throughout the decommissioning lifecycle
controls.lod_basis: First line of defense: organizational safety management system

supporting_text: MRR lacked an effective safety management system to identify and control hazards from reactive chemicals.
MRR's lack of robust process safety management systems and inconsistent operational practices made it difficult for the CSB to understand what specific reactive chemistry occurred.
It appears that neither the OSHA Process Safety Management (PSM) Standard nor the EPA Risk Management Plan (RMP) Rule applied to MRR's deodorizing process.
More robust safety management systems in place at MRR could have prevented both incidents as well as protected the safety of the workers and the public from the hazards of exploding pressure vessels.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
